title: AP World History: Modern MCQ Practice Exam 01

questions.prompt: A historian describes Song China using the following figures: “Between c. 1000 and c. 1200, the number of degree holders serving in government offices increased, while printed study manuals and commercial schools expanded in major cities.” Which broader development is best supported by this information?

choices.A: The decline of bureaucratic government in favor of hereditary military rule
choices.B: The growth of a merit-based scholar-official system supported by printing and education
choices.C: The replacement of Confucian learning by Buddhist monastic administration
choices.D: The abandonment of centralized rule in favor of independent merchant republics

questions.correctAnswerText: The growth of a merit-based scholar-official system supported by printing and education
questions.explanation: The increase in degree holders, printed manuals, and schools points to the Song civil service examination system and scholar-official bureaucracy.

questions.prompt: A historian describes Song China using the following figures: “Between c. 1000 and c. 1200, the number of degree holders serving in government offices increased, while printed study manuals and commercial schools expanded in major cities.” Which evidence would best support the claim that cultural traditions helped legitimize Song rule?

choices.A: The increased use of ocean-going junks in Southeast Asian trade
choices.B: The importation of Champa rice from Vietnam
choices.C: The growth of porcelain production for foreign markets
choices.D: The use of Confucian classics as the basis for civil service examinations

questions.correctAnswerText: The use of Confucian classics as the basis for civil service examinations
questions.explanation: Confucian texts shaped the exam system and helped legitimize Song bureaucratic rule.

questions.prompt: A historian describes Song China using the following figures: “Between c. 1000 and c. 1200, the number of degree holders serving in government offices increased, while printed study manuals and commercial schools expanded in major cities.” The development described most directly depended on which technological change?

choices.A: The spread of woodblock and movable-type printing
choices.B: The introduction of the spinning jenny
choices.C: The mechanization of textile mills
choices.D: The development of the telegraph

questions.correctAnswerText: The spread of woodblock and movable-type printing
questions.explanation: Printing made study materials more available, supporting education and examination preparation.

questions.prompt: A historian describes Song China using the following figures: “Between c. 1000 and c. 1200, the number of degree holders serving in government offices increased, while printed study manuals and commercial schools expanded in major cities.” Which broader economic context best helps explain the developments described?

choices.A: The collapse of long-distance trade across Afro-Eurasia
choices.B: The disappearance of urban markets in East Asia
choices.C: The commercialization and urban growth of the Song economy
choices.D: The end of agricultural production in southern China

questions.correctAnswerText: The commercialization and urban growth of the Song economy
questions.explanation: Commercial expansion and urban growth created demand for education, printing, and bureaucratic service.

questions.prompt: Described map: “A map of the western Indian Ocean c. 1300 shows arrows connecting Aden, Kilwa, Calicut, Hormuz, and Malacca. Seasonal arrows reverse direction between the Arabian Sea and the Indian subcontinent. Labels note Swahili-speaking merchants on the East African coast and Muslim merchant communities in western India.” Which development is most directly shown by the map?

choices.A: The domination of Indian Ocean commerce by landlocked Central Asian empires
choices.B: The isolation of East Africa from Afro-Eurasian trade
choices.C: The organization of maritime trade around monsoon wind patterns and port cities
choices.D: The decline of Islam as a commercial network in the Indian Ocean

questions.correctAnswerText: The organization of maritime trade around monsoon wind patterns and port cities
questions.explanation: The map’s seasonal arrows and port connections show how monsoon winds structured Indian Ocean commerce.

questions.prompt: Described map: “A map of the western Indian Ocean c. 1300 shows arrows connecting Aden, Kilwa, Calicut, Hormuz, and Malacca. Seasonal arrows reverse direction between the Arabian Sea and the Indian subcontinent. Labels note Swahili-speaking merchants on the East African coast and Muslim merchant communities in western India.” Which feature of the map best explains the spread of Islam along parts of the Indian Ocean rim?

choices.A: The presence of Muslim merchant communities in connected port cities
choices.B: The absence of urban centers in coastal regions
choices.C: The military conquest of all ports by a single caliphate
choices.D: The replacement of maritime trade by steppe caravan routes

questions.correctAnswerText: The presence of Muslim merchant communities in connected port cities
questions.explanation: Muslim merchant diasporas helped spread Islam through commercial and social connections.

questions.prompt: Described map: “A map of the western Indian Ocean c. 1300 shows arrows connecting Aden, Kilwa, Calicut, Hormuz, and Malacca. Seasonal arrows reverse direction between the Arabian Sea and the Indian subcontinent. Labels note Swahili-speaking merchants on the East African coast and Muslim merchant communities in western India.” Which environmental factor most directly shaped the trade pattern shown?

choices.A: The freezing of northern sea lanes for most of the year
choices.B: The spread of desertification across coastal India
choices.C: The annual flooding of the Nile River
choices.D: The predictable seasonal reversal of monsoon winds

questions.correctAnswerText: The predictable seasonal reversal of monsoon winds
questions.explanation: Indian Ocean sailors depended on seasonal wind reversals to plan voyages.

questions.prompt: Described map: “A map of the western Indian Ocean c. 1300 shows arrows connecting Aden, Kilwa, Calicut, Hormuz, and Malacca. Seasonal arrows reverse direction between the Arabian Sea and the Indian subcontinent. Labels note Swahili-speaking merchants on the East African coast and Muslim merchant communities in western India.” Which conclusion about diasporic communities is best supported by the map?

choices.A: They were limited to inland farming villages.
choices.B: They helped link distant ports through shared commercial and cultural practices.
choices.C: They ended the need for local political authorities in port cities.
choices.D: They prevented the movement of religious ideas across the ocean.

questions.correctAnswerText: They helped link distant ports through shared commercial and cultural practices.
questions.explanation: Diasporic merchant communities connected ports through trade, language, religion, and trust networks.

questions.prompt: Originalized imperial order, Ottoman Empire, late sixteenth century: “Let the registers be checked so that provincial cavalry holders provide service when called. Let village revenues be recorded carefully, and let no official collect beyond what is written, lest the subjects abandon their fields.” What was the most likely purpose of this order?

choices.A: To strengthen imperial control over taxation and military obligations
choices.B: To abolish all provincial administration
choices.C: To promote representative democracy in rural districts
choices.D: To end agricultural production in favor of factory labor

questions.correctAnswerText: To strengthen imperial control over taxation and military obligations
questions.explanation: The order regulates revenue records and cavalry service, showing an effort to manage provincial obligations.

questions.prompt: Originalized imperial order, Ottoman Empire, late sixteenth century: “Let the registers be checked so that provincial cavalry holders provide service when called. Let village revenues be recorded carefully, and let no official collect beyond what is written, lest the subjects abandon their fields.” The order reflects which common feature of early modern land-based empires?

choices.A: Reliance on elected legislatures to collect taxes
choices.B: Rejection of military elites in imperial expansion
choices.C: Use of bureaucratic records to manage revenue and service
choices.D: Dependence on industrial corporations to govern provinces

questions.correctAnswerText: Use of bureaucratic records to manage revenue and service
questions.explanation: The passage emphasizes registers, recorded revenues, and official limits, all features of imperial bureaucracy.

questions.prompt: Originalized imperial order, Ottoman Empire, late sixteenth century: “Let the registers be checked so that provincial cavalry holders provide service when called. Let village revenues be recorded carefully, and let no official collect beyond what is written, lest the subjects abandon their fields.” Which evidence from the passage most directly shows concern about the economic effects of overtaxation?

choices.A: The reference to cavalry holders
choices.B: The warning that subjects might abandon their fields
choices.C: The command to call soldiers for service
choices.D: The use of the phrase “imperial order”

questions.correctAnswerText: The warning that subjects might abandon their fields
questions.explanation: The warning links excessive collection to agricultural disruption and peasant flight.

questions.prompt: Originalized imperial order, Ottoman Empire, late sixteenth century: “Let the registers be checked so that provincial cavalry holders provide service when called. Let village revenues be recorded carefully, and let no official collect beyond what is written, lest the subjects abandon their fields.” Which broader context best explains why Ottoman rulers issued orders like this?

choices.A: The disappearance of large agrarian empires after 1450
choices.B: The collapse of Islamic legal traditions in Southwest Asia
choices.C: The spread of plantation slavery to Anatolia
choices.D: The need to govern diverse populations across a large territorial empire

questions.correctAnswerText: The need to govern diverse populations across a large territorial empire
questions.explanation: The Ottoman Empire needed administrative systems to manage varied provinces, subjects, taxes, and military obligations.

questions.prompt:
Table: Estimated change in selected crops and livestock after 1500
Region | New introduction | Reported effect by c. 1700
West Africa | Cassava | Expanded cultivation in some forest zones
Andes | Wheat and sheep | New colonial estates and grazing lands
Europe | Maize and potatoes | Population growth in some rural regions
Which conclusion is best supported by the table?

choices.A: The Columbian Exchange affected only European diets.
choices.B: New crops and animals had identical effects in every region.
choices.C: American crops reduced agricultural production globally.
choices.D: Biological exchange reshaped environments and economies in multiple regions.

questions.correctAnswerText: Biological exchange reshaped environments and economies in multiple regions.
questions.explanation: The table shows crops and animals moving across regions and changing agriculture, land use, and population patterns.

questions.prompt:
Table: Estimated change in selected crops and livestock after 1500
Region | New introduction | Reported effect by c. 1700
West Africa | Cassava | Expanded cultivation in some forest zones
Andes | Wheat and sheep | New colonial estates and grazing lands
Europe | Maize and potatoes | Population growth in some rural regions
Which limitation should a historian consider when using this table?

choices.A: It proves that all regions experienced the same demographic outcome.
choices.B: It summarizes broad patterns but does not show local variation or resistance.
choices.C: It contains no evidence of biological transfers.
choices.D: It describes only pre-1200 agricultural systems.

questions.correctAnswerText: It summarizes broad patterns but does not show local variation or resistance.
questions.explanation: The table gives general outcomes but not the complexity of local responses or uneven effects.

questions.prompt:
Table: Estimated change in selected crops and livestock after 1500
Region | New introduction | Reported effect by c. 1700
West Africa | Cassava | Expanded cultivation in some forest zones
Andes | Wheat and sheep | New colonial estates and grazing lands
Europe | Maize and potatoes | Population growth in some rural regions
The changes in the Andes were most closely connected to which process?

choices.A: The end of coerced labor in Spanish colonies
choices.B: The isolation of Andean communities from Atlantic empires
choices.C: The creation of colonial landholding and labor systems
choices.D: The decline of silver mining in the Americas before 1500

questions.correctAnswerText: The creation of colonial landholding and labor systems
questions.explanation: Wheat, sheep, estates, and grazing in the Andes were connected to Spanish colonial land and labor arrangements.

questions.prompt:
Table: Estimated change in selected crops and livestock after 1500
Region | New introduction | Reported effect by c. 1700
West Africa | Cassava | Expanded cultivation in some forest zones
Andes | Wheat and sheep | New colonial estates and grazing lands
Europe | Maize and potatoes | Population growth in some rural regions
Which claim is most directly supported by the table?

choices.A: Transoceanic connections created lasting changes in agriculture.
choices.B: Maritime empires ended all regional food traditions.
choices.C: Livestock introductions occurred only in Europe.
choices.D: American crops caused the immediate collapse of African states.

questions.correctAnswerText: Transoceanic connections created lasting changes in agriculture.
questions.explanation: The listed crop and livestock transfers show how oceanic exchange transformed agricultural systems.

questions.prompt: Originalized political pamphlet, Saint-Domingue, 1790s: “Those who labor under the sun know the meaning of liberty better than the men who write it and deny it. If rights are natural, they cannot stop at the plantation gate.” Which development most directly shaped the argument in the pamphlet?

choices.A: The Protestant Reformation
choices.B: The rise of fascist parties
choices.C: The spread of Enlightenment ideas about natural rights
choices.D: The creation of the United Nations

questions.correctAnswerText: The spread of Enlightenment ideas about natural rights
questions.explanation: The pamphlet uses the language of liberty and natural rights, central ideas in Atlantic revolutions.

questions.prompt: Originalized political pamphlet, Saint-Domingue, 1790s: “Those who labor under the sun know the meaning of liberty better than the men who write it and deny it. If rights are natural, they cannot stop at the plantation gate.” Which phrase best reveals the author’s criticism of social hierarchy?

choices.A: “They cannot stop at the plantation gate”
choices.B: “Those who labor under the sun”
choices.C: “Men who write it”
choices.D: “The meaning of liberty”

questions.correctAnswerText: “They cannot stop at the plantation gate”
questions.explanation: This phrase directly challenges the exclusion of enslaved plantation laborers from rights.

questions.prompt: Originalized political pamphlet, Saint-Domingue, 1790s: “Those who labor under the sun know the meaning of liberty better than the men who write it and deny it. If rights are natural, they cannot stop at the plantation gate.” The social conflict implied in the passage was most directly connected to which economic system?

choices.A: Pastoral nomadism in Central Asia
choices.B: Feudal labor obligations in medieval Europe
choices.C: State socialism in the Soviet Union
choices.D: Plantation slavery in the Atlantic world

questions.correctAnswerText: Plantation slavery in the Atlantic world
questions.explanation: Saint-Domingue’s revolutionary conflict was rooted in plantation slavery and Atlantic sugar production.

questions.prompt: Originalized political pamphlet, Saint-Domingue, 1790s: “Those who labor under the sun know the meaning of liberty better than the men who write it and deny it. If rights are natural, they cannot stop at the plantation gate.” Which statement best evaluates the pamphlet’s argument in its historical context?

choices.A: It rejected revolutionary ideals because enslaved workers supported monarchy.
choices.B: It used revolutionary language to challenge the contradiction between natural rights and slavery.
choices.C: It defended colonial plantation owners as protectors of liberty.
choices.D: It argued that citizenship should be restricted to European-born men.

questions.correctAnswerText: It used revolutionary language to challenge the contradiction between natural rights and slavery.
questions.explanation: The pamphlet applies natural-rights arguments to enslaved laborers, exposing the contradiction of revolutionary liberty in a slave society.

questions.prompt: Described visual source: “A late nineteenth-century illustrated magazine image shows a European official seated at a desk while African laborers carry bundles of rubber. In the background, armed guards stand near a river steamer. The caption praises ‘order and progress in the interior.’” Which interpretation is best supported by the image description?

choices.A: European imperialism relied entirely on voluntary wage labor.
choices.B: Claims of progress could be used to justify coercive imperial labor systems.
choices.C: African states controlled European commercial companies.
choices.D: Industrialization eliminated the use of violence in colonial economies.

questions.correctAnswerText: Claims of progress could be used to justify coercive imperial labor systems.
questions.explanation: The image pairs coercive labor and armed guards with a caption praising progress, revealing imperial justification.

questions.prompt: Described visual source: “A late nineteenth-century illustrated magazine image shows a European official seated at a desk while African laborers carry bundles of rubber. In the background, armed guards stand near a river steamer. The caption praises ‘order and progress in the interior.’” Which evidence would best support an argument that industrial demand encouraged imperial expansion?

choices.A: The spread of religious reform movements in Europe
choices.B: The persistence of small-scale subsistence farming
choices.C: The popularity of romantic poetry
choices.D: The extraction of rubber for use in industrial goods

questions.correctAnswerText: The extraction of rubber for use in industrial goods
questions.explanation: Rubber was a raw material demanded by industrial economies, encouraging imperial extraction.

questions.prompt: Described visual source: “A late nineteenth-century illustrated magazine image shows a European official seated at a desk while African laborers carry bundles of rubber. In the background, armed guards stand near a river steamer. The caption praises ‘order and progress in the interior.’” Which broader nineteenth-century development made the scene more possible?

choices.A: Steam-powered transportation and industrial manufacturing increased demand for raw materials.
choices.B: The decline of all European overseas empires after 1800
choices.C: The end of global capitalism before industrialization
choices.D: The disappearance of coercive labor in colonial settings

questions.correctAnswerText: Steam-powered transportation and industrial manufacturing increased demand for raw materials.
questions.explanation: River steamers and rubber extraction reflect industrial technology and demand for raw materials.

questions.prompt: Described visual source: “A late nineteenth-century illustrated magazine image shows a European official seated at a desk while African laborers carry bundles of rubber. In the background, armed guards stand near a river steamer. The caption praises ‘order and progress in the interior.’” Which feature most clearly suggests a biased imperial point of view?

choices.A: The presence of a river
choices.B: The inclusion of laborers
choices.C: The caption praising colonial rule as “order and progress”
choices.D: The use of a magazine format

questions.correctAnswerText: The caption praising colonial rule as “order and progress”
questions.explanation: The caption frames coercive colonial rule positively, revealing imperial bias.

questions.prompt:
Table: Mobilization during World War I
Region or empire | Soldiers or laborers mobilized outside the home region
British India | More than 1 million soldiers
French West Africa | Hundreds of thousands of soldiers and workers
Ottoman Arab provinces | Large numbers of conscripts and labor battalions
Which conclusion is best supported by the table?

choices.A: World War I drew colonial and imperial populations into a global conflict.
choices.B: World War I was fought only by European citizens within Europe.
choices.C: Colonized peoples were unaffected by wartime mobilization.
choices.D: Imperial armies disappeared before the war began.

questions.correctAnswerText: World War I drew colonial and imperial populations into a global conflict.
questions.explanation: The table shows soldiers and laborers mobilized from India, West Africa, and Ottoman Arab provinces.

questions.prompt:
Table: Mobilization during World War I
Region or empire | Soldiers or laborers mobilized outside the home region
British India | More than 1 million soldiers
French West Africa | Hundreds of thousands of soldiers and workers
Ottoman Arab provinces | Large numbers of conscripts and labor battalions
Which broader context best explains the mobilization shown?

choices.A: The replacement of empires by the United Nations before 1914
choices.B: The isolationist policies of all major states during the early twentieth century
choices.C: The existence of large imperial systems that could draw manpower from many regions
choices.D: The abolition of mass conscription in every empire

questions.correctAnswerText: The existence of large imperial systems that could draw manpower from many regions
questions.explanation: Empires mobilized colonial and provincial populations for war.

questions.prompt:
Table: Mobilization during World War I
Region or empire | Soldiers or laborers mobilized outside the home region
British India | More than 1 million soldiers
French West Africa | Hundreds of thousands of soldiers and workers
Ottoman Arab provinces | Large numbers of conscripts and labor battalions
Which claim about total war is most directly supported by the table?

choices.A: Total war reduced the role of state planning.
choices.B: Total war ended the need for labor forces.
choices.C: Total war was limited to naval blockades.
choices.D: Total war required states and empires to mobilize human resources on a large scale.

questions.correctAnswerText: Total war required states and empires to mobilize human resources on a large scale.
questions.explanation: The table shows mass mobilization of soldiers and workers beyond home fronts.

questions.prompt:
Table: Mobilization during World War I
Region or empire | Soldiers or laborers mobilized outside the home region
British India | More than 1 million soldiers
French West Africa | Hundreds of thousands of soldiers and workers
Ottoman Arab provinces | Large numbers of conscripts and labor battalions
The wartime experiences shown in the table most directly contributed after 1918 to which development?

choices.A: The permanent end of nationalist movements
choices.B: Increased demands for self-rule and political reform in some colonies
choices.C: The disappearance of anti-imperial activism
choices.D: The restoration of preindustrial forms of warfare

questions.correctAnswerText: Increased demands for self-rule and political reform in some colonies
questions.explanation: Colonial wartime service often intensified postwar expectations for rights, reform, or independence.

questions.prompt: Originalized speech by a West African nationalist leader, late 1950s: “Political freedom must not be a flag alone. It must mean control over our cocoa, our railways, our schools, and the planning of our future. We welcome friendship from all blocs, but we will not exchange one master for another.” Which purpose is most evident in the speech?

choices.A: To defend continued colonial administration
choices.B: To demand independence while avoiding domination by Cold War alliances
choices.C: To support direct rule by a foreign corporation
choices.D: To reject economic development after independence

questions.correctAnswerText: To demand independence while avoiding domination by Cold War alliances
questions.explanation: The speech connects political freedom, economic control, and refusal to submit to either Cold War bloc.

questions.prompt: Originalized speech by a West African nationalist leader, late 1950s: “Political freedom must not be a flag alone. It must mean control over our cocoa, our railways, our schools, and the planning of our future. We welcome friendship from all blocs, but we will not exchange one master for another.” Which phrase best supports the claim that economic sovereignty was central to the speaker’s nationalism?

choices.A: “We welcome friendship”
choices.B: “Control over our cocoa, our railways, our schools”
choices.C: “All blocs”
choices.D: “A flag alone”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Control over our cocoa, our railways, our schools”
questions.explanation: This phrase directly identifies economic and institutional control as part of independence.

questions.prompt: Originalized speech by a West African nationalist leader, late 1950s: “Political freedom must not be a flag alone. It must mean control over our cocoa, our railways, our schools, and the planning of our future. We welcome friendship from all blocs, but we will not exchange one master for another.” The final sentence most directly reflects which Cold War-era position?

choices.A: Fascism
choices.B: Militarism
choices.C: Nonalignment
choices.D: Mercantilism

questions.correctAnswerText: Nonalignment
questions.explanation: Welcoming friendship from all blocs while rejecting new masters reflects nonalignment.

questions.prompt: Originalized speech by a West African nationalist leader, late 1950s: “Political freedom must not be a flag alone. It must mean control over our cocoa, our railways, our schools, and the planning of our future. We welcome friendship from all blocs, but we will not exchange one master for another.” Which statement best evaluates the speaker’s argument?

choices.A: The speaker argues that formal independence is incomplete without political and economic self-determination.
choices.B: The speaker argues that independence should be postponed until industrialization is complete.
choices.C: The speaker argues that decolonization requires rejecting all international contact.
choices.D: The speaker argues that Cold War military alliances are the only path to sovereignty.

questions.correctAnswerText: The speaker argues that formal independence is incomplete without political and economic self-determination.
questions.explanation: The speech says freedom must be more than a flag and must include control over resources and planning.

questions.prompt:
Table: Approximate global container shipping capacity
Year | Index value, 1970 = 100
1970 | 100
1990 | 480
2010 | 2,300
Which conclusion is best supported by the table?

choices.A: Global trade became less dependent on transportation technology after 1970.
choices.B: Maritime trade disappeared as air freight replaced all shipping.
choices.C: Containerization helped expand the scale and speed of global commerce.
choices.D: Industrial production became entirely local by 2010.

questions.correctAnswerText: Containerization helped expand the scale and speed of global commerce.
questions.explanation: The sharp rise in shipping capacity reflects containerization’s role in global trade expansion.

questions.prompt:
Table: Approximate global container shipping capacity
Year | Index value, 1970 = 100
1970 | 100
1990 | 480
2010 | 2,300
The trend shown in the table most directly contributed to which development?

choices.A: The growth of global supply chains
choices.B: The end of international manufacturing
choices.C: The disappearance of multinational corporations
choices.D: The isolation of consumer markets from producers

questions.correctAnswerText: The growth of global supply chains
questions.explanation: Container shipping made it easier to coordinate production and distribution across regions.

questions.prompt:
Table: Approximate global container shipping capacity
Year | Index value, 1970 = 100
1970 | 100
1990 | 480
2010 | 2,300
Which environmental concern is most directly connected to the trend shown?

choices.A: The end of fossil-fuel use in transportation
choices.B: The disappearance of coastal infrastructure
choices.C: The decline of global resource extraction
choices.D: Increased emissions and ecological pressures from expanded global transport

questions.correctAnswerText: Increased emissions and ecological pressures from expanded global transport
questions.explanation: Expanded shipping capacity is connected to fossil-fuel use, port expansion, and environmental impacts.

questions.prompt:
Table: Approximate global container shipping capacity
Year | Index value, 1970 = 100
1970 | 100
1990 | 480
2010 | 2,300
Which social development was facilitated by the same late twentieth-century global integration reflected in the table?

choices.A: The end of migration across national borders
choices.B: The spread of consumer goods and cultural products across regions
choices.C: The complete disappearance of local identities
choices.D: The return to isolated subsistence economies

questions.correctAnswerText: The spread of consumer goods and cultural products across regions
questions.explanation: The same global integration that expanded shipping also helped circulate goods and cultural products.

questions.prompt: Secondary-source interpretation: “In the Andes before 1450, rulers expanded authority not only through armies but also through roads, storehouses, labor obligations, and ritual claims that linked communities to sacred landscapes.” Which development does this interpretation best describe?

choices.A: The creation of a maritime trading league in the Caribbean
choices.B: The use of administrative and religious systems to integrate Andean communities
choices.C: The spread of Islam through West African caravan cities
choices.D: The emergence of industrial wage labor in South America

questions.correctAnswerText: The use of administrative and religious systems to integrate Andean communities
questions.explanation: Roads, storehouses, labor obligations, and sacred landscapes show administrative and religious integration.

questions.prompt: Secondary-source interpretation: “In the Andes before 1450, rulers expanded authority not only through armies but also through roads, storehouses, labor obligations, and ritual claims that linked communities to sacred landscapes.” A later empire using similar strategies would most likely be represented by which visual or material source?

choices.A: A steam locomotive used to transport factory workers
choices.B: A printed nationalist newspaper calling for parliamentary elections
choices.C: A container ship unloading manufactured goods
choices.D: A record-keeping device or road system used to organize labor and tribute

questions.correctAnswerText: A record-keeping device or road system used to organize labor and tribute
questions.explanation: Such material evidence would illustrate administrative integration and labor organization in an Andean-style empire.

questions.prompt: Secondary-source interpretation: “In the Andes before 1450, rulers expanded authority not only through armies but also through roads, storehouses, labor obligations, and ritual claims that linked communities to sacred landscapes.” Which phrase best supports the claim that state power depended on more than military conquest?

choices.A: “Not only through armies”
choices.B: “Before 1450”
choices.C: “In the Andes”
choices.D: “Expanded authority”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Not only through armies”
questions.explanation: The phrase directly indicates that military force was only one part of state-building.

questions.prompt: Secondary-source interpretation: “In the Andes before 1450, rulers expanded authority not only through armies but also through roads, storehouses, labor obligations, and ritual claims that linked communities to sacred landscapes.” Which limitation should a historian recognize in this interpretation?

choices.A: It shows exact tax rates for every Andean community.
choices.B: It proves that Andean states had no religious authority.
choices.C: It summarizes general state-building strategies but does not provide the views of conquered communities.
choices.D: It demonstrates that Andean rule depended entirely on oceanic commerce.

questions.correctAnswerText: It summarizes general state-building strategies but does not provide the views of conquered communities.
questions.explanation: A secondary overview may explain structures while leaving out the perspectives of subject peoples.

questions.prompt: Described map: “A map of Afro-Eurasia c. 1350 marks caravan routes across Central Asia, sea routes through the Indian Ocean, and overland routes linking North Africa to West Africa. Dots mark cities such as Samarkand, Cairo, Timbuktu, Calicut, and Quanzhou.” Which conclusion is best supported by the map?

choices.A: Multiple overlapping trade networks linked distant regions of Afro-Eurasia.
choices.B: Trade was limited to a single route controlled by one empire.
choices.C: West Africa had no connection to wider commercial networks.
choices.D: Maritime trade completely replaced overland trade by 1350.

questions.correctAnswerText: Multiple overlapping trade networks linked distant regions of Afro-Eurasia.
questions.explanation: The map includes caravan, maritime, and trans-Saharan routes connecting many cities.

questions.prompt: Described map: “A map of Afro-Eurasia c. 1350 marks caravan routes across Central Asia, sea routes through the Indian Ocean, and overland routes linking North Africa to West Africa. Dots mark cities such as Samarkand, Cairo, Timbuktu, Calicut, and Quanzhou.” Which later land-based empire most directly used Central Asian routes and steppe traditions as part of its expansion and administration?

choices.A: The Aztec Empire
choices.B: The Portuguese maritime empire
choices.C: The Qing Empire
choices.D: The Kingdom of Kongo

questions.correctAnswerText: The Qing Empire
questions.explanation: The Qing expanded into Inner Asia and used Central Asian and steppe political-military traditions.

questions.prompt: Described map: “A map of Afro-Eurasia c. 1350 marks caravan routes across Central Asia, sea routes through the Indian Ocean, and overland routes linking North Africa to West Africa. Dots mark cities such as Samarkand, Cairo, Timbuktu, Calicut, and Quanzhou.” If the map were created by a merchant from Samarkand, which point of view might most influence what the map emphasized?

choices.A: A desire to show the decline of all inland commerce
choices.B: An interest in caravan cities, safe routes, and commercial connections across Central Asia
choices.C: A belief that Atlantic plantations dominated all trade before 1450
choices.D: A focus on industrial railroads and coal production

questions.correctAnswerText: An interest in caravan cities, safe routes, and commercial connections across Central Asia
questions.explanation: A Samarkand merchant would likely emphasize inland trade routes and caravan connections.

questions.prompt: Described map: “A map of Afro-Eurasia c. 1350 marks caravan routes across Central Asia, sea routes through the Indian Ocean, and overland routes linking North Africa to West Africa. Dots mark cities such as Samarkand, Cairo, Timbuktu, Calicut, and Quanzhou.” Which change after 1450 most altered the balance among the networks shown?

choices.A: The end of all commerce between Asia and Europe
choices.B: The disappearance of the Indian Ocean trade
choices.C: The closure of all caravan routes across Central Asia
choices.D: The expansion of European-sponsored transoceanic maritime routes

questions.correctAnswerText: The expansion of European-sponsored transoceanic maritime routes
questions.explanation: After 1450, European maritime expansion altered global trade patterns and intensified transoceanic connections.

questions.prompt: Secondary-source interpretation: “Iberian and Dutch overseas empires differed in institutions, but both used armed ships, chartered privileges, and local alliances to turn oceanic mobility into political power.” Which evidence would best support the interpretation?

choices.A: The decline of all port cities after 1500
choices.B: The refusal of European states to support overseas trade
choices.C: The replacement of commerce by pastoral nomadism
choices.D: The granting of monopoly rights to companies or officials in overseas territories

questions.correctAnswerText: The granting of monopoly rights to companies or officials in overseas territories
questions.explanation: Chartered privileges and monopoly rights supported political and commercial power overseas.

questions.prompt: Secondary-source interpretation: “Iberian and Dutch overseas empires differed in institutions, but both used armed ships, chartered privileges, and local alliances to turn oceanic mobility into political power.” Which visual source would most directly illustrate the technological basis of the process described?

choices.A: A photograph of a twentieth-century assembly line
choices.B: A drawing of a cannon-armed ocean-going ship approaching a fortified port
choices.C: A poster encouraging women to work in wartime factories
choices.D: A chart showing internet access in the twenty-first century

questions.correctAnswerText: A drawing of a cannon-armed ocean-going ship approaching a fortified port
questions.explanation: Armed ships were central to converting maritime mobility into coercive imperial power.

questions.prompt: Secondary-source interpretation: “Iberian and Dutch overseas empires differed in institutions, but both used armed ships, chartered privileges, and local alliances to turn oceanic mobility into political power.” Which phrase most directly supports a comparison between different European imperial systems?

choices.A: “Oceanic mobility”
choices.B: “Political power”
choices.C: “Differed in institutions”
choices.D: “Local alliances”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Differed in institutions”
questions.explanation: This phrase directly frames a comparison between Iberian and Dutch imperial structures.

questions.prompt: Secondary-source interpretation: “Iberian and Dutch overseas empires differed in institutions, but both used armed ships, chartered privileges, and local alliances to turn oceanic mobility into political power.” Which broader historical context best explains the interpretation?

choices.A: European states and companies competed for access to Asian, African, and American trade networks.
choices.B: The Atlantic world became isolated from all other regions.
choices.C: Maritime empires avoided cooperation with local elites.
choices.D: Oceanic empires developed before improvements in navigation and shipbuilding.

questions.correctAnswerText: European states and companies competed for access to Asian, African, and American trade networks.
questions.explanation: The interpretation fits the broader context of European maritime competition after 1450.

questions.prompt:
Table: Social reform legislation in selected regions, c. 1820–1880
Region | Reform trend
Britain | Restrictions on child labor and expansion of male suffrage
Ottoman Empire | Legal reforms promising equal treatment of subjects
Japan | Abolition of formal status privileges after the Meiji Restoration
Which conclusion is best supported by the table?

choices.A: Reform movements were limited to the Atlantic revolutions.
choices.B: Nineteenth-century states used legal reform to address social and political pressures.
choices.C: Industrialization ended the need for political reform.
choices.D: All reforms created full democracy immediately.

questions.correctAnswerText: Nineteenth-century states used legal reform to address social and political pressures.
questions.explanation: The table shows different states adopting reforms in response to pressures from industrialization, empire, and modernization.

questions.prompt:
Table: Social reform legislation in selected regions, c. 1820–1880
Region | Reform trend
Britain | Restrictions on child labor and expansion of male suffrage
Ottoman Empire | Legal reforms promising equal treatment of subjects
Japan | Abolition of formal status privileges after the Meiji Restoration
Which limitation should a historian recognize when using the table?

choices.A: It shows that reforms were unrelated to state power.
choices.B: It proves that all subjects experienced reform equally.
choices.C: It covers only events before 1450.
choices.D: It lists reform trends but does not show how effectively reforms were implemented.

questions.correctAnswerText: It lists reform trends but does not show how effectively reforms were implemented.
questions.explanation: A table of laws or trends cannot by itself prove enforcement or lived outcomes.

questions.prompt:
Table: Social reform legislation in selected regions, c. 1820–1880
Region | Reform trend
Britain | Restrictions on child labor and expansion of male suffrage
Ottoman Empire | Legal reforms promising equal treatment of subjects
Japan | Abolition of formal status privileges after the Meiji Restoration
Which argument is most directly supported by the table?

choices.A: Reform could occur in both industrializing and non-Western imperial contexts.
choices.B: Reform was always caused by Marxist revolutions.
choices.C: Reform movements rejected legal change as a strategy.
choices.D: Reform ended social hierarchy in every society.

questions.correctAnswerText: Reform could occur in both industrializing and non-Western imperial contexts.
questions.explanation: Britain, the Ottoman Empire, and Japan show reform across different political and regional settings.

questions.prompt:
Table: Social reform legislation in selected regions, c. 1820–1880
Region | Reform trend
Britain | Restrictions on child labor and expansion of male suffrage
Ottoman Empire | Legal reforms promising equal treatment of subjects
Japan | Abolition of formal status privileges after the Meiji Restoration
Which development most directly pressured Britain to restrict child labor?

choices.A: The spread of nomadic pastoralism
choices.B: The abolition of all wage labor
choices.C: Industrial factory production and urban social criticism
choices.D: The decline of mechanized textile manufacturing

questions.correctAnswerText: Industrial factory production and urban social criticism
questions.explanation: Child labor restrictions in Britain arose from industrial working conditions and reform criticism.

questions.prompt: Described photograph, c. 1905: “A dockside image shows South Asian migrants boarding a steamship under contract to work on sugar estates in the Caribbean. A noticeboard lists medical inspection, contract length, and return-passage rules.” Which claim is best supported by the photograph description?

choices.A: Migration after 1750 was limited to voluntary tourism.
choices.B: Steamships ended all labor migration.
choices.C: Industrial-era labor systems created regulated long-distance migration flows.
choices.D: Contract labor replaced all forms of imperial economic control.

questions.correctAnswerText: Industrial-era labor systems created regulated long-distance migration flows.
questions.explanation: The photograph description shows contracted workers, steamship travel, inspections, and return rules.

questions.prompt: Described photograph, c. 1905: “A dockside image shows South Asian migrants boarding a steamship under contract to work on sugar estates in the Caribbean. A noticeboard lists medical inspection, contract length, and return-passage rules.” Which later development most directly challenged similar systems of imperial control over labor and mobility?

choices.A: Decolonization movements demanding political and social self-determination
choices.B: The revival of mercantilist chartered companies in the 1600s
choices.C: The expansion of Mongol tribute networks
choices.D: The introduction of silver currency in Ming China

questions.correctAnswerText: Decolonization movements demanding political and social self-determination
questions.explanation: Decolonization challenged imperial systems that had regulated labor, mobility, and political rights.

questions.prompt: Described photograph, c. 1905: “A dockside image shows South Asian migrants boarding a steamship under contract to work on sugar estates in the Caribbean. A noticeboard lists medical inspection, contract length, and return-passage rules.” Which twentieth- and twenty-first-century development shows continuity with the migration pattern suggested by the photograph?

choices.A: The complete end of labor migration after decolonization
choices.B: The disappearance of migrant communities in former plantation regions
choices.C: The elimination of remittances from the global economy
choices.D: Continued movement of workers across regions in response to global labor demand

questions.correctAnswerText: Continued movement of workers across regions in response to global labor demand
questions.explanation: Modern labor migration continues the pattern of workers moving across regions because of global economic demand.
